title: S3-like Object Storage — System Design
description: Design a massively scalable, extremely durable object store: PUT and GET arbitrary blobs by key, store exabytes, and never lose data. Four themes carry the design: splitting the metadata plane from the data plane, durability via replication or erasure coding, multipart upload for huge objects, and the ongoing maintenance (repair and GC) that keeps durability true over time.

nodes.label: Client / SDK
nodes.description: PUTs and GETs objects by bucket/key; does multipart for large objects.

nodes.label: Front-End / API
nodes.description: Authenticates, routes, and rate-limits S3-style requests.

nodes.label: Metadata Service
nodes.description: Maps bucket/key -> object version -> chunk/placement locations.

nodes.label: Metadata Store
nodes.description: Authoritative object metadata, versions, and locations, sharded by key.

nodes.label: Placement Service
nodes.description: Chooses which storage nodes hold an object's data (across failure domains).

nodes.label: Storage Data Nodes
nodes.description: Disks that store object data chunks / shards durably.

nodes.label: Data-Plane Gateway (Replication / Erasure Coder)
nodes.description: Data-plane gateway: clients stream object bytes to it; it replicates or erasure-codes them across data nodes and returns a durable placement receipt to the Metadata Service.

nodes.label: Multipart Upload Coordinator
nodes.description: Tracks parts of an in-progress large upload until completed.

nodes.label: Repair / Scrubber
nodes.description: Detects lost/corrupt shards and rebuilds them to restore durability.

nodes.label: Garbage Collector
nodes.description: Reclaims space from deleted objects and abandoned uploads.

nodes.label: Bucket Index
nodes.description: Lists keys in a bucket (prefix listing), derived from metadata.

functional: PUT an object (bucket + key + bytes) and GET it back; DELETE and HEAD by key.
List objects in a bucket by prefix with a delimiter and a pagination cursor.
Support very large objects via multipart upload (initiate / upload part / complete / abort).
Object versioning (optional, per-bucket): versioned reads, delete markers, latest-pointer semantics.
Per-tenant/bucket authorization on every operation; per-bucket quotas.
Idempotent, conditional writes (content checksum, idempotency key, If-Match/If-None-Match).
nonFunctional: Extreme durability (e.g. 11 nines) — effectively never lose data, maintained over time via repair.
High availability for GET/PUT, degrading to reconstruction/alternate replicas under node loss.
Scale to trillions of objects and exabytes across many failure domains.
Storage-efficient (control the cost of durability — hybrid replication/EC by object class).
Strong consistency for GET/HEAD AND for LIST: a successful PUT/DELETE is immediately reflected in both a read and a prefix listing.
Encryption at rest (envelope/KMS) and in transit; tenant isolation; auditable access.

Scope: this design delivers multi-zone durability within one region. Cross-region replication and regional disaster recovery are an explicit extension (see follow-ups), not part of the mainline durability promise.

capacity.label: Objects
capacity.note: At ~1 KB metadata each ≈ 10 PB logical metadata before indexes/replicas — must shard heavily (Step 7).

capacity.label: Total bytes
capacity.note: Across thousands of data nodes / many zones.

capacity.label: Object size
capacity.value: avg ~4 MB, p95 ~64 MB, range bytes→TBs
capacity.note: ~70% of objects are small (<1 MB) but most bytes live in large objects — multipart needed above ~100 MB.

capacity.label: Peak PUT/s
capacity.note: Drives metadata write capacity + index update throughput.

capacity.label: Peak GET/s
capacity.note: ~10:1 read:write; hot objects CDN/cache-fronted to offload data nodes.

capacity.label: Peak LIST/s
capacity.note: Served from the derived ordered bucket index, not the point store.

capacity.label: Peak DELETE/s
capacity.note: Logical delete (metadata) is cheap; reclamation is async GC.

capacity.label: Durability target
capacity.note: Drives EC/replication scheme AND repair speed — see repair budget below.

capacity.label: Storage overhead
capacity.note: Hybrid keeps the exabyte tier near 1.4× raw instead of 3×.

capacity.label: Repair budget
capacity.note: Repair must outrun further failures or the durability math breaks; throttled to a fraction of node bandwidth.

capacity.label: Metadata footprint
capacity.note: 10T objects × ~1 KB; replicated/indexed copies multiply it. Sized in Step 7.

capacity.label: Failure domains
capacity.note: Spread copies/shards across them; hot-bucket skew can be 100×+ the average — hash-shard + cache.

api.description: Store an object. The Metadata Service coordinates: authorize + admission-check (quota), reserve an idempotency record and an invisible write intent, pick placement; the client streams bytes to the data-plane gateway which replicates/erasure-codes them durably and returns a placement receipt; only then does the Metadata Service commit the version + latest pointer + bucket index + quota counters in one transaction (read-after-write). Headers carry a content checksum and optional idempotency key so a retried PUT after a client timeout returns the same versionId instead of duplicating.
api.request: (object bytes; headers: Authorization, content-type, x-checksum-sha256, optional x-idempotency-key, optional If-None-Match: * for create-only, optional x-storage-class, optional x-sse-kms-key-id)

messages.label: commit txn: version + latest + index + quota + idempotency result

api.description: Retrieve an object by key. Defaults to the latest version; '?versionId=v_3' reads a specific version (and returns 404 if the latest is a delete marker and no versionId is given). A 'Range: bytes=0-1048575' header returns just that byte range as 206 Partial Content — common for resumable downloads and for reading an object's tail without fetching the whole blob.
api.request: (bucket/key in path; optional ?versionId=v_3; optional Range: bytes=start-end; Authorization)
api.response: 200 (full object) or 206 (range) with Content-Length, ETag, x-version-id, x-checksum-sha256; or 404

api.description: Return object metadata (size, etag, version, content-type, storage class) without the body. Same strong consistency as GET.
api.response: 200 headers: Content-Length, ETag, x-version-id, x-storage-class, x-checksum-sha256 (or 404 / 404+delete-marker)

api.description: Delete an object. On a versioned bucket a DELETE without versionId writes a delete marker (latest read returns 404) and keeps prior versions; DELETE ?versionId= permanently removes that version. On a non-versioned bucket it logically deletes; bytes are reclaimed asynchronously by GC.
api.request: Headers: Authorization (SigV4-style), optional x-idempotency-key, optional If-Match. Optional ?versionId=

api.description: List keys in a bucket by prefix. delimiter='/' rolls keys up into common prefixes (pseudo-directories); cursor paginates; results are strongly consistent with prior PUT/DELETE (the bucket index is part of the metadata commit). Range reads via 'Range:' header are supported on object GET.
api.request: Query: prefix, delimiter, cursor (opaque continuation token), max-keys. Header: Authorization.

api.description: Initiate a multipart upload. Returns an uploadId the client uses to upload parts and then complete or abort.
api.request: Headers: Authorization, content-type, optional x-storage-class, optional x-sse-kms-key-id (encryption key reference).

api.description: Upload one part of a multipart upload. Parts are uploaded independently and in parallel; each is stored durably and returns an etag. Re-uploading the same partNumber replaces it (idempotent at part granularity).
api.request: (part bytes; headers: x-checksum-sha256 for the part). Min part size enforced except the last part.

api.description: Complete the multipart upload. The server validates the submitted part list (numbers, sizes, etags) against stored parts, assembles the object, and commits its metadata atomically exactly once — the object becomes visible only here. An idempotency key makes a retried complete return the same versionId.

messages.label: 400 (no commit; parts stay until abort/TTL GC)

api.description: Abort an in-progress multipart upload. Marks the upload aborted so GC reclaims its parts after a TTL; uncompleted uploads are also reaped by a TTL even without an explicit abort.

dataModel.name: buckets
dataModel.note: Tenant-owned namespace + policy/quota state. Authorization and admission checks read this on every operation.

dataModel.name: object_versions
dataModel.note: One row per (bucket, key, version_id), sharded by bucket/key hash. A row is created in state=pending_data (a write intent, invisible) and only becomes visible when object_latest/bucket_index point at it in the commit txn; after commit the content fields are immutable. Delete markers are versions with is_delete_marker=true.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(pending_data, data_durable, committed, deleting, gc_eligible, reclaimed)

dataModel.name: object_latest
dataModel.note: Per (bucket, key) pointer to the current version (or a delete marker). Atomically flipped on PUT/DELETE so reads see one consistent version.

dataModel.name: bucket_index
dataModel.note: Derived, key-ordered listing index, range-partitioned by (bucket, key) and adaptively split at hot points. Updated in the SAME (cross-shard) metadata commit as object_latest, so LIST is strongly consistent with PUT/DELETE. Pagination is a live range scan; the opaque cursor is the last (key, version) returned, so each page reflects commits up to when it is served.

fields.name: visible
fields.type: bool (false if latest is a delete marker)

dataModel.name: multipart_uploads
dataModel.note: In-progress large uploads. Reaped by GC on abort or after a lifecycle TTL.

dataModel.name: multipart_parts
dataModel.note: One row per uploaded part; validated against the submitted list at complete.

fields.name: size / etag / checksum
fields.type: validated at complete (min size except last)

dataModel.name: shard_placements (on data nodes)
dataModel.note: The actual bytes: replicas or erasure-coded fragments. Scrubbed and repaired against the metadata placement.

fields.name: placement_epoch
fields.type: which placement map this shard belongs to

dataModel.name: quarantined_shards
dataModel.note: Shards that failed a scrub or read-time checksum. Quarantined shards are never served or counted as live redundancy until repaired or evicted.

dataModel.name: repair_jobs
dataModel.note: Queue of redundancy to restore, prioritized by how close an object is to losing its last spare. Throttled and isolated from serving traffic.

fields.name: placement_epoch
fields.type: guards against rebuilding a superseded placement

dataModel.name: gc_marks
dataModel.note: Mark phase of GC mark/sweep. A placement is sweepable only if its mark still matches the metadata observed at mark time AND no live version references it AND retention/legal-hold has passed.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Dedupes retried PUT / complete / DELETE after client timeouts, mapping a key to the result it already produced.

patterns.name: Metadata/data plane split
patterns.what: A scalable metadata service maps keys to locations; data nodes store the bytes.
patterns.whenToUse: Blob stores where metadata and bulk data scale and behave differently.

patterns.name: Replication for durability
patterns.what: Store N copies across failure domains so any node loss is survivable.
patterns.whenToUse: Simplicity and fast reads; higher storage overhead.

patterns.name: Erasure coding
patterns.what: Split data into k data + m parity fragments; any k reconstruct it — durability at far lower overhead.

patterns.name: Tiered redundancy by object class
patterns.what: Replicate hot/small objects (fast, cheap repair) and erasure-code cold/large ones (low overhead), migrating between tiers via a lifecycle policy as objects cool.
patterns.whenToUse: The chosen durability policy: it keeps exabyte storage near 1.4× while keeping hot reads/repair fast.

patterns.name: Commit-metadata-last
patterns.what: Write data durably first, then commit metadata, so readers never see an object whose data is missing.

patterns.name: Multipart upload
patterns.what: Upload a large object as independent parts, then atomically complete.
patterns.whenToUse: Huge objects and resumable, parallel uploads.

patterns.name: Scrub + repair + GC
patterns.what: Continuously verify checksums, rebuild lost shards, and reclaim deleted/abandoned space.
patterns.whenToUse: Maintaining durability and cost over time on failing hardware.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Store the Blob on One Fileserver (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works: a single fileserver with a disk. PUT writes the object's bytes to a local file named by bucket/key; GET reads that file back. For a handful of objects this is a correct, complete object store.

It fails the two headline requirements immediately, and the capacity numbers show why each ceiling is independent. Durability: one disk holds the only copy, so a disk failure — routine at scale — loses the object outright, nowhere near 11 nines. Scale: one server's disk caps total bytes (we need exabytes), its filesystem caps object count (we need trillions), and its NIC caps throughput (we need ~300k PUT/s and ~3M GET/s). No single box clears any of the three.

So the baseline motivates the design. To never lose data you need multiple copies on independent hardware (replication / erasure coding), and to scale you must separate the small, queryable mapping (which object lives where) from the bulk bytes so each scales on its own. The next two steps introduce exactly that split and that redundancy.

view.caption: The client/SDK sends PUT/GET to the Front-End / API and reads/writes the object bytes directly against the single set of Storage Data Nodes — no separate data-plane gateway yet.

steps.decisionPrompt: All objects live as files on one disk. Which two requirements does that violate the instant a disk fails or the server fills up?
whyNow: Starting from one fileserver makes the two pressures concrete: a single copy isn't durable (so we add redundancy) and one box doesn't scale (so we split metadata from data) — the next two steps.

recap.after: Objects stored as files on a single fileserver.
recap.newRisk: A single disk is neither durable (one failure loses data) nor scalable (one box caps bytes/throughput).

traps.trap: Treating a single fileserver (or one NAS) as 'good enough' object storage.
traps.why: One disk/host is a single point of data loss and a hard capacity/throughput ceiling — it can't approach 11 nines or exabyte scale.
traps.instead: Split metadata from data and store each object redundantly across independent failure domains.

steps.title: 2. Split Metadata from Data
description: Two very different problems: a tiny, queryable mapping (bucket/key -> where the bytes live) and the bulk bytes themselves. Keep them in separate planes. The metadata service owns the key->location/version mapping in a sharded transactional store; data nodes store the actual object bytes.

Metadata is authoritative for existence and for control: it's the source of truth for whether an object exists, and it's where authorization (bucket policy / tenant ownership) and quota admission are enforced on every operation. A reader always resolves metadata first; raw bytes on a data node mean nothing without a committed metadata pointer at them.

A request resolves metadata first (authz, locations, version), then streams bytes to/from the data plane. This lets metadata be consistent and heavily sharded (by bucket/key hash) while raw storage scales independently and cheaply across thousands of disks. Everything queryable (listing, versions, placement, ownership, encryption key reference) lives in metadata; the data plane just stores and serves opaque (encrypted) bytes.

The invariant to state out loud: data nodes never decide object visibility. Only a committed metadata row — the object version plus its latest pointer and bucket-index entry — makes an object exist for readers. Bytes that are durable on disk but not yet pointed at by committed metadata are simply orphans, invisible to GET, HEAD, and LIST until (and unless) the metadata commits.

view.caption: The Front-End / API resolves each object through the Metadata Service backed by the Metadata Store, while the client/SDK streams the bytes separately to the Storage Data Nodes.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why separate a metadata service from the nodes that hold the bytes?

concepts.definition: A consistent key->location index separate from the bulk byte storage.
concepts.whyItMatters: Each plane scales and is consistent on its own terms; metadata stays small and queryable.
concepts.example: Metadata DB sharded by key hash; petabytes of bytes on commodity data nodes.

whyNow: The plane split is the architectural backbone; durability, multipart, and listing all build on this separation.

recap.after: Key->location metadata separate from opaque byte storage.
recap.newRisk: A single copy of the bytes on one node isn't durable — disks and nodes fail.

steps.title: 3. Durability: Replication vs Erasure Coding
description: Disks and nodes fail constantly, so a single copy loses data. Two schemes give durability. Replication: store N full copies across distinct failure domains (racks/zones); any copy serves reads, and losing up to N-1 is survivable — simple and fast but N× storage.

Erasure coding: split the object into k data + m parity fragments spread across k+m nodes; any k fragments reconstruct it. This tolerates m losses at far lower overhead (e.g. 10+4 = 1.4× vs 3× replication) — the standard for large/cold data, at the cost of reconstruction work on read/repair.

The cost gap is the whole reason this is a decision: 1 EB logical at 3× replication is 3 EB of raw disk, while the same exabyte erasure-coded 10+4 is ~1.4 EB — roughly 1.6 EB saved on the cold tier alone. Replicating the hot tier raises the blended multiplier above 1.4×, but because most bytes are cold, the blended overhead stays far closer to 1.4× than to 3×.
Spread copies/fragments across independent failure domains so a rack or zone loss can't take enough of them to lose the object — this placement is what actually delivers the durability target.

view.caption: The Metadata Service asks the Placement Service where to store, which has the Replication / Erasure Coder encode and write shards across the Storage Data Nodes.

options.name: Tiered: replicate hot/small, erasure-code cold/large (default)
options.description: A lifecycle policy classifies objects by class: replicate hot/small ones (3×) for fast cheap repair and low read latency, and erasure-code cold/large ones (e.g. 10+4 ≈ 1.4×) for low overhead, migrating from replicas to EC as objects cool. This is what real stores do, and it keeps the exabyte tier near 1.4× instead of 3×.
pros: Best of both: fast cheap-repair replication for hot/small objects, low-overhead EC for the large/cold bytes that dominate the bill.
Storage cost tracks the access pattern instead of one blanket policy — exabytes stay near 1.4×.
Objects transition tiers (replicated → erasure-coded) as they cool, via the lifecycle policy.
cons: Two redundancy code paths to build, test, and operate.
Needs a classifier/lifecycle policy to decide and migrate object classes.
Tier transitions move bytes and rewrite redundancy, consuming background bandwidth.

view.caption: The Placement Service replicates hot/small objects directly to Storage Data Nodes while routing cold/large ones through the Replication / Erasure Coder to write shards.

options.name: Erasure coding everywhere (k+m fragments)
options.description: Each object is split into k data plus m parity fragments spread across failure domains, and any k of them reconstruct it, tolerating m losses — applied uniformly to all objects.
pros: Far lower storage overhead than copies — e.g. 10+4 is ~1.4x while tolerating any 4 fragment losses.
Hits 11 nines on large/cold data without paying 3x storage everywhere.
Fragments spread across failure domains so a rack/zone loss can't take enough to lose the object.
cons: A read may gather k fragments, adding latency versus reading one whole copy.
Repairing one lost fragment reads many others (repair amplification), costing bandwidth.
Encode/decode CPU on write and reconstruction; awkward and slow for tiny hot objects.

view.caption: The Placement Service has the Replication / Erasure Coder split each object into k+m fragments and write the shards across many Storage Data Nodes.

options.name: Full N-way replication everywhere
options.description: N complete copies of each object are stored across distinct failure domains; any single copy serves a read and a lost copy is replaced by copying a survivor.
pros: Simplest scheme: any single copy serves a read with no reconstruction.
Cheap single-object repair — just copy from a surviving replica.
Lowest read latency, ideal for small/hot objects.
cons: N× storage (3 copies = 3×) is wasteful for cold petabytes.
Same durability needs more raw disks than erasure coding.
At exabyte scale the storage cost dominates the bill.

view.caption: The Placement Service writes N full copies of the object directly across distinct Storage Data Nodes, skipping the erasure coder.

steps.decisionPrompt: Replication or erasure coding? How do you hit 11 nines without paying 3x storage everywhere?

concepts.definition: N full copies on distinct failure domains.
concepts.whyItMatters: Simple, fast reads, survives N-1 losses — but N× storage.

concepts.definition: k data + m parity fragments; any k reconstruct the object.
concepts.whyItMatters: Same/better durability at much lower storage overhead.

whyNow: Durability is the headline requirement; the replication-vs-EC tradeoff (and cross-domain placement) is the central senior decision.

recap.after: Tiered durability — replicate hot/small, erasure-code cold/large — across failure domains; target met near 1.4× overhead.
recap.newRisk: A reader could see an object whose metadata committed before its data finished — ordering matters.

traps.why: Two copies on the same rack die together; the math of 11 nines assumes independent failures.

steps.title: 3a. Replication vs Erasure Coding
description: Both achieve the durability target; the choice is cost vs read/repair behavior. Replication keeps N full copies: simple, fast reads from any copy, and cheap single-object repair — but N× storage (e.g. 3× for 3 copies).

Erasure coding splits an object into k data + m parity fragments where any k reconstruct it: far less overhead (e.g. 10+4 ≈ 1.4× for 4-failure tolerance) — but a read may need to gather k fragments, and repairing one lost fragment reads many others (repair amplification).

Real stores use both: replication for small/hot objects (read latency matters, overhead is small in absolute terms) and erasure coding for large/cold objects (storage cost dominates). Place fragments/copies across independent failure domains either way.

view.caption: The Replication / Erasure Coder writes the object's encoded shards across n Storage Data Nodes.

steps.decisionPrompt: 11 nines without 3× storage everywhere: where do you use replication vs erasure coding?

concepts.definition: Replication = N copies (simple, fast, N× storage); EC = k+m fragments, any k reconstruct (low overhead, read/repair amplification).
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the central durability-cost decision; the right answer is usually 'both, by object class'.
concepts.example: Hot small objects replicated 3×; large cold objects erasure-coded 10+4 (~1.4×).

traps.why: 3× replication for cold petabytes is wasteful; EC for tiny hot objects adds read/repair latency.
traps.instead: Replicate hot/small, erasure-code large/cold; spread across failure domains regardless.

recap.after: Replication vs EC chosen per object class with failure-domain placement.
recap.newRisk: EC repair amplification and gather-on-read latency — bounded by class assignment + repair throttling.

steps.title: 4. Read-After-Write via Commit Ordering
description: With two planes, ordering is the consistency lever. The Metadata Service is the write coordinator: it authorizes, picks placement, has the data written durably to its nodes first (replicas/fragments acknowledged via a durable placement receipt), then commits the metadata pointing to it last. The object becomes visible only once both exist, and the coordinator owns retries after a client timeout.

Be precise about which rows exist before visibility. On a PUT the coordinator first reserves an idempotency record and writes a short-lived write-intent row (a pending object_versions row in state pending_data, with its reserved version_id, placement plan, and a TTL). That intent is NOT visible: object_latest and bucket_index do not point at it, so GET/HEAD/LIST cannot see it. Visibility is conferred only by the commit transaction that flips the latest pointer and index entry — the 'write intent / visibility pointer' is the single mental model that ties commit-last PUT, multipart complete, delete markers, LIST, idempotent retry, and GC together.

The write state machine is explicit: pending_data (intent reserved) → data_durable (shards acked, placement receipt) → committed (one MetaDB txn: version row finalized + latest pointer flipped + bucket index updated + bucket usage/quota counters incremented + idempotency result stored) → deleting/delete_marker → gc_eligible → reclaimed. Because version, latest, and index commit together, GET and LIST observe the same version. A crash before 'committed' leaves only an invisible intent + orphan data (no live pointer), which the intent TTL and GC reclaim — never a dangling pointer to missing bytes.

Crash outcomes by boundary: (a) before data durable — only the intent + idempotency reservation exist; nothing is visible, GC/intent-TTL reclaim. (b) after data durable but before metadata commit — durable orphan shards with no live pointer; still invisible, reclaimed once the placement is proven unreferenced. (c) after commit but before the client receives the 200 — the object IS visible and durable; the client retries with the same idempotency key and the coordinator returns the already-committed versionId. No boundary produces a visible object whose bytes are missing.

This gives read-after-write for GET/HEAD and for LIST. Retried PUTs are deduplicated by an idempotency key so a client that times out and retries gets the same versionId instead of a duplicate object. Versioning fits cleanly: a new PUT creates a new version and atomically flips the latest pointer; a DELETE on a versioned bucket writes a delete marker as the new latest.

view.caption: The Metadata Service resolves objects against the Metadata Store while the Replication / Erasure Coder writes shards to the Storage Data Nodes, with the metadata commit ordering reads after writes.

options.name: Commit metadata last (default)
options.description: A PUT writes the object's data to its nodes and waits for durable acknowledgement, then commits the metadata pointer last, so the object is visible only once both exist.
pros: Read-after-write: a successful PUT means data is durable AND metadata committed, so an immediate GET resolves.
A crash mid-PUT leaves only orphan data (no pointer), which GC reclaims — never a dangling pointer.
Versioning fits cleanly: write new version's data, then atomically flip the 'latest' pointer.
cons: Orphan data accumulates from failed PUTs until GC runs.
The PUT isn't visible until the final metadata commit, so perceived write latency includes both phases.
Requires the data write to be durably acknowledged before committing — an extra round of confirmation.

view.caption: A PUT streams bytes first to the Replication / Erasure Coder (durable on the Storage Data Nodes), then the Metadata Service commits the metadata pointer last to the Metadata Store.

options.name: Commit metadata first, then write data
options.description: A PUT records the metadata entry for the key first and then streams the object's bytes to the data nodes afterward.
pros: The object is recorded immediately, so the namespace reflects the intended write right away.
Simpler client flow: register the key, then stream bytes to it.
No orphan data — every data write already has a metadata entry.
cons: A reader can resolve metadata pointing at bytes that aren't durable yet — a dangling pointer / 404 on the data.
A crash after metadata but before data leaves corruption visible to readers, not reclaimable orphans.
Breaks the stated read-after-write guarantee.

view.caption: A PUT commits the metadata entry to the Metadata Store first via the Metadata Service, then streams the object bytes to the Replication / Erasure Coder afterward.

steps.decisionPrompt: On a PUT, do you commit metadata before or after the data is durable? What breaks each way?

concepts.definition: Persist data durably, then commit the metadata pointer.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees readers never resolve an object whose bytes aren't stored; gives read-after-write.
concepts.example: Write 3 replicas -> commit metadata -> PUT returns -> GET succeeds.

concepts.definition: A pending, invisible object_versions row (state pending_data) reserved before the bytes land; visibility is conferred only when the commit txn flips the latest pointer and bucket index to it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Names the boundary between 'exists on disk' and 'visible to readers' — the single idea behind commit-last PUT, multipart complete, delete markers, idempotent retry, and GC safety.
concepts.example: pending_data intent + idempotency reservation -> data durable -> one txn: version+latest+index+quota+idempotency -> object visible.

whyNow: Read-after-write is a stated requirement; commit ordering is the precise mechanism and the subtle two-plane correctness point.

recap.after: Read-after-write (GET + LIST); crashes leave reclaimable orphans, not corruption.
recap.newRisk: Very large objects can't be a single streamed PUT — they need multipart.

failureDrills.scenario: Crash before data is durable (boundary a): intent reserved, shards not acked.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Nothing is visible — only the pending_data intent and idempotency reservation exist. The intent TTL expires and GC reclaims any partial shards; a retry with the same idempotency key re-drives the write cleanly.
failureDrills.mitigation: Write-intent row with TTL + idempotency reservation; no latest/index pointer was ever written.

failureDrills.scenario: Crash after data durable but before metadata commit (boundary b): coordinator dies holding a durable placement receipt.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: No version is visible; the durable shards carry no live metadata pointer and are reclaimed by GC after their placement is proven unreferenced. A retry reuses the durable shards and commits.
failureDrills.mitigation: Commit-metadata-last + GC mark/sweep against committed placements + intent TTL.

failureDrills.scenario: Crash after commit but before the client sees the 200 (boundary c), then the client retries.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The object is already visible and durable; the idempotency key matches the committed result, so the retry returns the same versionId instead of creating a duplicate version.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotency result stored inside the commit txn; the coordinator returns the stored result on retry.

failureDrills.scenario: Duplicate retry with the same idempotency key while the first PUT is still in flight.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The second request sees the reserved idempotency record and either waits for or returns the first request's outcome — never a second version or second placement.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotency record reserved (per account, key) before placement; the coordinator owns completion.

flows.name: PUT: data durable first, metadata committed last
flows.note: Bytes are written and acknowledged durable before the metadata pointer commits, so a reader never resolves an object whose data is missing.

messages.label: commit txn: version + latest + index + quota + idempotency
5xx (no commit; intent TTL + GC reclaim orphan bytes)

steps.title: 5. Multipart Upload for Large Objects
description: A multi-GB/TB object can't be one atomic streamed request (failures waste everything, and it ties up a connection). Initiate a multipart upload to get an uploadId, then PUT independent parts (in parallel, resumable), each durably stored and acknowledged with an etag and recorded in a part record (number, size, checksum, placement). Re-uploading a part number replaces it, so retries are idempotent at part granularity.

Complete validates before it commits: the submitted part list must match stored parts on number, etag/checksum and minimum size (all but the last), and the upload must be in_progress. Only then does the coordinator assemble the object and commit its metadata atomically exactly once — guarded by an idempotency key so a retried complete returns the same versionId. The object appears only on completion.

One ETag subtlety worth stating: for a multipart object the final ETag is NOT a hash of the whole object. Following S3, it's a digest-of-digests — the hash of the concatenated per-part checksums plus a '-N' part-count suffix (e.g. '...-37') — which is cheap to compute without re-reading the assembled object but means ETag ≠ full-object SHA-256. For end-to-end integrity, clients rely on the explicit x-checksum-sha256 (per part and, if requested, a full-object composite checksum), not on ETag equality.
Abort is first-class: it marks the upload aborted so GC reclaims its parts; uploads that are never completed or aborted are reaped after a lifecycle TTL, so failed large uploads don't leak storage.

view.caption: The Front-End / API streams multipart parts to the Multipart Upload Coordinator, which persists each part via the Replication / Erasure Coder and commits the object to the Metadata Service on complete.

steps.decisionPrompt: A 5 TB upload fails at 80%. How do you avoid restarting from zero?

concepts.definition: Upload a large object as independent parts, then atomically complete.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables parallel, resumable large uploads and atomic visibility on completion.
concepts.example: Initiate -> PUT parts 1..N (resumable) -> complete -> object visible.

whyNow: Multipart is the standard mechanism for large objects and reuses the commit-last visibility rule at part granularity — a natural extension.

recap.after: Parallel, resumable large uploads completed atomically.
recap.newRisk: Durability degrades over time as disks fail and deletes/abandoned uploads pile up.

flows.name: Multipart upload: parts then atomic complete
flows.note: Each part is stored and acked independently (resumable); the object becomes visible only when complete commits metadata.

steps.title: 6. Scrubbing, Repair, and Garbage Collection
description: Durability isn't a one-time write property — disks rot and nodes die continuously. A background scrubber reads shards on a cadence (e.g. every shard re-verified within a 2-week cycle), recomputes checksums, and quarantines any shard that fails verification so a corrupt copy is never served or counted as live before it's replaced. The repair process rebuilds missing/quarantined replicas/fragments (re-replicate, or re-encode from surviving fragments) to restore the target redundancy.

Repair speed matters: the durability math assumes lost redundancy is restored before further failures accumulate, so repair is prioritized (objects closest to losing their last redundancy first) and parallelized across many source/target nodes. Repair bandwidth is throttled to a fraction of node bandwidth and isolated from serving traffic so a big rebuild doesn't starve GET/PUT — targets are roughly < 30 min to re-protect a lost node and < 4 h for a lost rack.

Garbage collection reclaims space safely under one invariant: a placement is reclaimed only when (1) no live latest or retained version references it, (2) retention window and legal hold have passed, AND (3) the placement generation/epoch still matches the metadata row observed during marking. The mark/sweep marks candidate placements, then re-checks reachability and epoch at sweep time, so a placement that became referenced again by a concurrent PUT between mark and sweep is skipped — it never deletes bytes a live version still points at. It also reaps abandoned multipart parts and expired write-intent (pending_data) rows past their TTL — keeping cost bounded without risking live data.

view.caption: The Repair / Scrubber scrubs and rebuilds shards on the Storage Data Nodes using placement from the Metadata Service, while the Garbage Collector finds deleted entries in the Metadata Store and reclaims space on the nodes.

steps.decisionPrompt: A data node with millions of shards dies. What restores durability, and how fast must it be?

concepts.definition: Continuously verify checksums and rebuild lost redundancy from survivors; bad shards go to quarantined_shards and rebuilds are tracked in repair_jobs, prioritized by remaining redundancy.
concepts.whyItMatters: Maintains the durability target as hardware fails over time.
concepts.example: Lost a fragment of a 10+4 object -> quarantine + queue repair_job -> re-encode from the surviving 10+.

concepts.definition: Mark/sweep that reclaims a placement only if it is unreferenced by any live/retained version, past retention/legal-hold, and its placement epoch still matches the metadata seen at mark time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets GC free space aggressively without ever racing a concurrent PUT into deleting live bytes.
concepts.example: Mark placement P (epoch 7) -> concurrent PUT re-references P at epoch 8 -> sweep sees epoch mismatch -> skip P.

whyNow: Ongoing repair/GC is what keeps durability and cost true over time; without it the 11-nines claim decays — this is the operational heart of the case.

recap.after: Continuously verified, repaired, and garbage-collected storage.
recap.newRisk: Listing trillions of keys and serving reads at scale still need to be cheap and available.

failureDrills.scenario: A whole rack of data nodes is lost.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Objects remain readable from copies/fragments in other domains; repair rebuilds the lost redundancy with priority, ordered by how close each object is to losing its last spare.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cross-domain placement + prioritized, throttled parallel repair before further loss.

failureDrills.scenario: A GET resolves placement but one shard fails its checksum / is missing.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The read falls back to another replica, or for an EC object gathers k other fragments and reconstructs the bytes; the bad shard is quarantined and a repair is queued. The client still gets correct data.
failureDrills.mitigation: Degraded read (alternate replica / EC reconstruction) + checksum quarantine + repair enqueue.

failureDrills.scenario: GC is about to free a shard that a live version still references.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: GC refuses: it only reclaims placements unreferenced by any committed metadata and past the retention window, so live data is never deleted even under races with a concurrent PUT.
failureDrills.mitigation: Mark/sweep against committed placements + placement-epoch check + retention guard.

flows.name: Degraded read: shard fails checksum, reconstruct + quarantine + enqueue repair
flows.note: Metadata resolves placement; one shard is missing or fails its checksum; the read falls back to other replicas/fragments, reconstructs the correct bytes, quarantines the bad shard, and enqueues a repair job — the client still gets correct data.

messages.label: a shard is missing / checksum mismatch
gather k surviving fragments / alternate replica
quarantine bad shard + enqueue repair job

steps.title: 7. Listing, Sharding, and Availability at Scale
description: Metadata is the scaling pressure point: shard the metadata store by bucket/key hash across many partitions so trillions of objects and PUT/GET resolution stay fast. (At ~10T objects × ~1 KB that's ~10 PB of metadata before indexes/replicas — far past one box.) Replicate each shard for availability and read-after-write.

Prefix listing ('list keys under foo/') is served from a derived bucket index ordered by key — a flat key space with delimiter-based prefix queries (S3 has no real directories). To keep LIST strongly consistent with PUT/DELETE, the bucket index row is updated in the SAME metadata commit transaction that flips the latest pointer. Object rows are hash-sharded by (bucket, key) while the bucket index is range-partitioned by (bucket, key) for ordered scans, so the two generally live on different shards. That leaves two ways to make the single commit atomic. (1) Use cross-shard transactions (FoundationDB/Spanner-style): the write commits the object shard and the index partition together. (2) Co-locate object_latest and bucket_index for a given (bucket, key) in the same shard by deriving both partitions from the same key — but then point GET resolution shares the bucket's index shard. We choose cross-shard transactions so point access stays hash-distributed while LIST stays strongly consistent.

Pagination is a live, key-ordered range scan, not a snapshot: a page returns committed rows in [cursor, end) as of when that page is read, and the opaque cursor is the last (key, version) returned. So a key committed before you reach its position appears; a key deleted before you reach it does not; concurrent writes/deletes to ranges you've already passed show up only if the client re-lists. This is the S3 contract — no isolated point-in-time snapshot of a trillion-key bucket, but every individual page is strongly consistent with commits up to the moment it is served.

A hot bucket or skewed key prefix (one tenant writing photos/2026/... at 100×+ the average) concentrates writes and listings on one index range. Mitigate it three ways: adaptively split the bucket's index range at hot split points so it repartitions under load, cap per-partition write fanout, and cache hot listing ranges — falling back to a per-bucket sequencer only when ordering across a split genuinely requires it. Object rows stay hash-sharded, so point GET/PUT is unaffected by index skew.

Keep the data path highly available: any replica/enough fragments serve a GET, a failed/corrupt shard falls back to an alternate replica or EC reconstruction, requests route around failed nodes, and hot objects can be cached/CDN-fronted. The metadata + data planes scale and fail independently.

view.caption: The Metadata Service reads and writes the hash-sharded Metadata Store and updates the key-ordered Bucket Index, while the Front-End / API resolves objects and clients stream data to the Storage Data Nodes.

options.name: Derived ordered bucket index, committed with metadata (default)
options.description: A key-ordered index partitioned by bucket, updated in the same metadata commit transaction that flips the latest pointer, serves prefix listings as a cheap ordered range scan with delimiter-based common-prefix queries — strongly consistent with PUT/DELETE.
pros: Prefix listing ('list keys under foo/') is a cheap ordered range scan over the index.
Strong list-after-write: committing the index in the same transaction as the latest pointer means a just-written/deleted key is immediately correct in listings.
Delimiter-based common-prefix queries work over a flat key space without real directories, with opaque-cursor pagination.
cons: The index is a second derived structure to maintain, and coupling it into the commit makes the write path do more work.
A hot bucket concentrates listing load on its index range — mitigate with caching and splitting hot index ranges.
Cross-shard buckets need the index partitioned by bucket while objects are hash-sharded by key, so the commit spans the object shard and the bucket's index partition.

view.caption: The Metadata Service keeps a key-ordered Bucket Index in sync with the Metadata Store so the Front-End / API can serve prefix listings as a range scan.

options.name: List by scanning the sharded metadata directly
options.description: Prefix listings scatter-gather across every metadata shard and merge the results, with no separate index to maintain.
pros: No separate index to build or keep in sync — one source of truth.
Listings are always current with the latest writes.
Less storage and fewer moving parts.
cons: Keys are hash-sharded for point access, so a prefix scan must hit every shard and merge results.
Listing a prefix becomes a scatter-gather over all partitions — slow and load-heavy at trillions of objects.
Couples expensive scans to the same store serving latency-sensitive PUT/GET.

view.caption: The Front-End / API resolves listings by having the Metadata Service scatter-gather across the sharded Metadata Store directly, with no separate index.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you list keys under a prefix when there are trillions of objects and no real directories?
whyNow: Closing on metadata sharding, listing, and availability shows the whole store scales and stays available — the remaining non-functional requirements.

concepts.definition: Keys are flat strings; 'directories' are prefix queries with a delimiter, served from a range-partitioned ordered bucket index committed in the same cross-shard transaction as the object's latest pointer, so listing is strongly consistent. Pagination is a live ordered range scan with an opaque (key, version) cursor — each page reflects commits up to when it is served, not a frozen snapshot.
concepts.whyItMatters: Scales listing without a real filesystem tree, and keeps LIST as consistent as GET while point access stays hash-sharded.
concepts.example: List delimiter='/' prefix='photos/' returns photos/ keys + common prefixes, including a key PUT a millisecond ago; the next page continues from the cursor.
concepts.group: Listing, Sharding, and Availability at Scale

recap.after: Sharded metadata, strongly-consistent prefix listing (index committed with the object), HA data path with degraded reads.
recap.newRisk: Metadata hot shards (a hot bucket) and repair bandwidth — bounded by hashing, caching, and throttled repair.

bottlenecks.issue: A hot bucket/prefix concentrates metadata load.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Hash-shard the key space; cache hot metadata; CDN-front hot objects.
Throttle and prioritize repair; isolate repair bandwidth.

bottlenecks.issue: A hot bucket/prefix concentrates listing writes on one ordered index range.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Adaptively split the index range at hot points; cap per-partition write fanout; cache hot listing ranges.

bottlenecks.issue: Background repair/index/lifecycle work can starve foreground requests.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Run repair/GC/tier-transition off a separate durable work queue with its own bandwidth/IO budget, isolated from PUT/GET.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Object Store
finalDesign.description: A metadata service (sharded by bucket/key, replicated) is the write coordinator: it authorizes against bucket policy, enforces per-tenant quotas, and maps keys to versions and placement. On a PUT the client streams bytes to a stateless data-plane gateway that replicates/erasure-codes them across failure domains and returns a durable placement receipt; GETs read shards back from the data nodes directly. Data nodes hold bytes under a tiered redundancy policy — replicate hot/small objects (3×) and erasure-code cold/large ones (~1.4×), migrating tiers as objects cool. The coordinator reserves an idempotency record and an invisible write intent, waits for the durable placement receipt, then commits the version + latest pointer + bucket index + quota counters in one transaction, giving read-after-write for GET, HEAD, and LIST; the explicit write state machine is pending_data → data_durable → committed → deleting/delete_marker → gc_eligible → reclaimed, where visibility is conferred only at the commit step. Large objects use multipart upload, validated and committed exactly once on complete. A scrubber + repair process continuously verify checksums, quarantine bad shards, and rebuild lost redundancy under throttled, prioritized repair; GC reclaims only unreferenced placements past retention plus abandoned uploads. Data at rest is envelope/KMS-encrypted; access is authorized and audited. The metadata and data planes scale and remain available independently, with degraded reads (alternate replica / EC reconstruction) and hot objects cached/CDN-fronted.

view.caption: On a PUT the client/SDK calls the Front-End / API to resolve metadata, then streams bytes to the data-plane gateway (Replication / Erasure Coder), which writes shards to the Storage Data Nodes and returns a durable placement receipt so the Metadata Service can commit last; GETs read bytes back from the data nodes. Large objects go through the Multipart Upload Coordinator, and the Repair/Scrubber, Garbage Collector, and Bucket Index keep data durable, reclaimed, and listable.

functional.how: Logical metadata delete / delete marker (GC reclaims later); strongly-consistent ordered bucket index committed with the object.
Multipart upload: part records validated, committed exactly once on complete; abort/TTL GC.
Immutable version rows + atomic latest pointer; DELETE writes a delete marker.
Commit version + latest + index in one txn after data is durable; idempotency-keyed.
Bucket policy checked and per-tenant quota admission-controlled on every operation by the metadata coordinator.
Idempotency key dedupes retried PUT/complete/DELETE; If-Match/If-None-Match guard conditional writes.

nonFunctional.how: Replication/erasure coding across failure domains + repair.
Tiered redundancy: replicate hot/small, erasure-code cold/large (~1.4×).
Replicated metadata; any copy/enough fragments serve reads; degraded reads reconstruct around failed/corrupt shards.
Envelope/KMS encryption at rest with per-object key reference; TLS in transit; per-bucket authorization and audit.

tradeoff: Self-hosting FoundationDB/TiKV gives strong transactions across the version+latest+index commit, but you operate the sharding, repair, and upgrades yourself.
Managed stores (DynamoDB/Spanner) remove ops but constrain transaction scope and can dominate cost at trillions of small rows.
makesIrrelevant: A managed auto-sharding transactional store removes most of the hand-rolled metadata partitioning logic in Step 7.

selfHosted: Custom log-structured store on bare disks
tradeoff: Building on raw disks lets you control erasure-coding scheme, placement, and repair throttling precisely (the whole point of this interview).
If a managed object store (S3/GCS/Blob) is acceptable, most of this design collapses to using it — choose self-host only when you need the control or the unit economics at exabyte scale.
makesIrrelevant: Using a managed object store as the data plane makes Steps 3–6 (EC, repair, GC) someone else's problem.

tradeoff: A durable log (Kafka) gives replayable repair/GC work and tier-transition events; a managed queue removes broker ops but caps retention/throughput tuning.
A CDN offloads hot-object GETs from data nodes and metadata; self-hosting a cache tier is only worth it for private/regional traffic the CDN can't serve.
Envelope encryption with a managed KMS keeps per-object data keys cheap to rotate; self-hosting Vault/HSM is for strict key-custody or air-gapped requirements.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability (durability risk, repair lag, orphan bytes, index lag)
tradeoff: Durability is invisible until it's gone — you must alert on objects below target redundancy, repair backlog age, scrub coverage, orphan/GC bytes, hot-prefix pressure, and checksum failures, not just request errors.

Round it out with SLO signals: p99 GET/PUT latency, LIST latency, quota-rejection rate, KMS failure rate, and degraded-read rate — so you see user-visible regressions and security/quota friction alongside the durability internals.

say: Confirm PUT/GET/HEAD/DELETE/prefix-list, multipart, optional versioning, authz + quotas, idempotent/conditional writes.
Pin extreme durability and read-after-write (state it covers GET, HEAD, AND LIST) as the hard goals.
Establish exabyte/trillion-object scale across failure domains, and put rough numbers on PUT/GET/LIST rates and object-size mix.
Split metadata plane from data plane; metadata is authoritative for visibility and enforces authz + quotas; data nodes never decide visibility.

Data path: client streams bytes to a stateless data-plane gateway that replicates/erasure-codes them and returns a durable placement receipt; GET reads shards back from data nodes. Tiered durability: replicate hot/small, erasure-code cold/large, across failure domains.
Metadata service is the write coordinator: reserve idempotency + write intent, data durable first, then commit version + latest + index + quota in one txn (read-after-write for GET and LIST).
Replication vs erasure coding tradeoff + placement.
Scrub/repair/GC; metadata sharding and prefix listing.
Map requirements to mechanisms; call out the write-intent/visibility-pointer model, the write state machine, and GC/repair safety invariants.
Tradeoffs: tiered replication vs EC (cost vs read/repair), repair speed vs serving, strong-LIST cross-shard commit cost and live-cursor semantics, metadata hot shards.
State the regional scope (multi-zone, single-region) and name encryption/KMS, cross-region replication/DR, lifecycle/retention/legal-hold, and object lock as extensions.

expectations: Splits metadata from data and replicates.
May miss erasure coding, commit ordering, and repair.
Designs tiered replication/EC across failure domains.
Commit-metadata-last as an explicit write state machine for read-after-write; idempotent retries.
Multipart with validated exactly-once complete + scrub/repair/GC with safety invariants.
Reasons about EC schemes and repair-speed vs durability math with real numbers.
Designs metadata sharding and strongly-consistent prefix listing at scale, with cursor semantics.
Threads authz, quotas, encryption, degraded reads, and hot-shard mitigation precisely; states GC non-reachability proof.

followUps: Replication vs erasure coding — when does each win, and what's the read/repair cost of EC?
How fast must repair be to maintain 11 nines, and why?
How do you make prefix LIST strongly consistent with PUT/DELETE over a flat, sharded key space, and what's the cost?
How does a retried PUT or multipart complete after a client timeout avoid duplicating the object?
How does GC prove a shard is safe to delete without ever removing live data?
How would you add per-tenant authorization, quotas, and envelope/KMS encryption?
How would you add cross-region replication and lifecycle (tiering/expiry/retention/legal-hold) policies?
How do you keep a hot object/bucket from overloading one metadata shard or data node?
